facts.text: Bernal Diaz del Castillo was born in Medina del Campo, a small town in the province of Leon.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was of a respectable family.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was an hidalgo, which signified descent from Christian forefathers without any mixture of Jewish or Moorish blood.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo first left Castile for the New World in the year 1514.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo first arrived in Mexico in the year 1519.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was born between 1495 and 1500.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo completed his work in the year 1568.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was about seventy years of age when he completed his work in 1568.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo reached the advanced age of eighty-six.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was endowed with singular nobleness of mind.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo enjoyed an unblemished reputation.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was a man devoted to his religion.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was the least superstitious of all the Spanish historians on the Conquest.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was a regidor of the town of Santiago in Guatimala.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was present at every battle and hostile encounter he described.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo boasted of having been in a hundred and nineteen battles.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo wrote his history from his day-book and memory.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo completed his history on the 26th day of February, 1568.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was an eyewitness to the conquest of New Spain.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo believed that all the merit of the conquest was not due to Cortes alone.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo generally used the expression 'Cortes and all of us' to distribute credit for the conquest.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo's writing style was chiefly characterized by plainness and simplicity.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo wrote passages with great force and eloquence.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo devoted chapters to describing his companions in arms by name.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo described the qualities and colours of the horses used in the conquest.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo considered the details about horses to be of the utmost importance to his history.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo's account is acknowledged to be the only one on which reliance can be placed regarding the conquest.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo's memoirs were pillaged by court historians after his death.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was never rewarded for the great services he rendered his country while living.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo declared and affirmed that what he wrote in his book was strictly true.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo pointed out errors and blunders in a work written by Francisco de Gomara.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo requested that printers not take away from nor add one single syllable to his narrative.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo departed from Castile in 1514.
He departed in the suite of Pedro Arias de Avila.
Pedro Arias de Avila had been appointed governor of Terra Firma.
During the sea voyage, the weather was sometimes bad and sometimes good.
They arrived at Nombre Dios.
The plague was raging at Nombre Dios.
Many men were lost to the plague.
Most of the men got terrible sores on their legs.
Dissensions arose between the governor and Vasco Nuñez de Balboa.
Vasco Nuñez de Balboa was a wealthy cavalier.
Vasco Nuñez de Balboa had brought the province to subjection.
Vasco Nuñez de Balboa was married to one of the daughters of Avila.
Suspicion had been excited against Vasco Nuñez de Balboa.
Vasco Nuñez de Balboa had formed a plan to make a voyage to the South Sea at his own expense.
Vasco Nuñez de Balboa required a considerable body of troops for his planned voyage.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo departed from Castile in the year 1514.
The expedition experienced both bad and good weather at sea.
They arrived at Nombre Dios where plague was raging.
Many men were lost to plague during their arrival.
Most of the men developed terrible sores on their legs.
Vasco Nuñez de Balboa was a wealthy cavalier who had brought the province to subjection.
Balboa was married to one of Pedro Arias de Avila's daughters.
Balboa formed a plan to make a voyage to the South Sea at his own expense.
Pedro Arias de Avila deposed Balboa and sentenced him to decapitation.
Other soldiers rebelled against their superior officers.
The island of Cuba had been conquered.
Diego Velasquez of Quellar was appointed governor of Cuba.
Diaz and others requested permission from Pedro Arias de Avila to proceed to Cuba.
Pedro Arias de Avila granted them permission.
They embarked in a good vessel and departed for Cuba.
Their voyage to Cuba was prosperous.
Diego Velasquez received them with great kindness.
Velasquez promised them the first Indians that might be discharged.
Three years passed without receiving the promised Indians.
One hundred ten men from Terra Firma met with others from Cuba to concert measures.
They chose Francisco Hernandez de Cordoba as their captain.
They purchased three vessels for their expedition.
Diego Velasquez gave them a third vessel on condition they invade the Guanajas islands.
Velasquez wanted three cargoes of Indians from the Guanajas islands as slaves.
Diaz and his men refused to enslave a free people.
They believed neither God nor the king commanded them to turn free people into slaves.
Diego Velasquez accepted their refusal and furnished them with provisions.
They purchased pigs at three pesos each.
There were neither cows nor sheep on the island of Cuba at that time.
Every soldier took glass beads for barter.
Anton de Alaminos was their principal pilot and a native of Palos.
Camacho de Triana and Juan Alvarez el Manquillo of Huelva were their other pilots.
They hired sailors and furnished themselves with ropes, anchors, and water-casks at their own expense.
One hundred ten men departed from a harbour called Ajaruco on the north coast of Cuba.
The distance from Ajaruco to San Christoval was twenty-four miles.
Havannah had been in their possession for only two years.
They engaged a priest named Alonso Gonzalez at San Christoval.
They appointed Bernardino Miguez as treasurer, a native of Saint Domingo de la Calzada.
They heard mass before departing on their voyage.
They sailed in the year 1517 from the harbour of Jaruco.
After twelve days' sail they passed the coast of Saint Antonius.
They steered continually towards the west.
They were ignorant of the shoals and currents in that latitude.
A terrible storm lasted two days and two nights.
They nearly perished in the storm.
They came in sight of land on the twenty-first day after departure from Cuba.
The country they discovered had never been discovered before.
They perceived a considerable sized town about six miles from the sea shore.
They named the town Grand Cairo because of its magnitude.
On the 5th of March they perceived five large canoes full of men approaching.
The canoes were hollowed out of the trunks of large trees.
The canoes could hold from forty to fifty Indians each.
More than thirty Indians climbed on board their principal ship.
They gave the Indians bacon and cassave bread to eat.
They presented each Indian with a necklace of green glass beads.
The chief was a cazique who wished to return home and come back the next day.
The Indians wore cotton cloaks and aprons called maltates.
The Indians of Yucatan were more intelligent than the Indians of Cuba.
The cazique said 'con escotoch' meaning 'come with me to my houses yonder.'
They called the spot Punta de Cotoche.
Their captain held a consultation about whether to go on shore.
They resolved to lower their boats and proceed with the twelve canoes to shore.
They marched in close order with their arms ready for action.
They took fifteen crossbows and a like number of matchlocks.
The cazique called his warriors from ambush to attack them.
Fifteen of their men were immediately wounded in the initial arrow attack.
The Indians were clad in cotton cuirasses and armed with lances, shields, bows, and slings.
The Indians wore tufts of feathers on their heads.
The Indians' lances were held in both hands.
The Indians began to give way when they felt the sharp edge of Spanish swords.
Fifteen of the Indians lay dead on the field.
There was a small place with three houses built of stone and lime.
These houses were temples containing many idols made of clay.
Some idols had the countenances of devils, others of females.
Some idols represented Indians committing horrible offences.
They found small wooden boxes containing other gods with hellish faces.
They found several small shells and ornaments in the temples.
They found three crowns in the temples.
The ornaments were made of an inferior kind of gold.
They felt overjoyed at the discovery of this country.
Peru was not discovered until sixteen years after this discovery.
The priest Gonzalez ordered the gold and small idols to be removed to their ships.
Two Indians from Cuba helped remove the gold and idols.
They took two native prisoners during the skirmish.
The two prisoners were subsequently baptised and became Christians.
One prisoner was named Melchior and the other Julian.
Both prisoners were tattooed about the eyes.
They resolved to re-embark and prosecute their voyage further west.
They dressed the wounds of their men before setting sail again.
They discovered many promontories, bays, reefs, and shallows.
They all considered the country to be an island.
Anton de Alaminos persisted that it was an island.
They proceeded with caution during daytime but lay to at nights.
After sailing for fourteen days they perceived another village of considerable magnitude.
There was a bay with an inner harbour.
They needed fresh water as their supply was becoming very scarce.
Their water casks were not of good quality.
The expedition was fitted out solely by persons in poor circumstances.
It was Sunday Lazari when they landed.
They named the place in honour of Sunday Lazari.
The Indians called the land Campeachy.
They determined to go on shore in their smallest vessel and three boats.
The sea in the bays was very shallow.
Their vessels anchored more than three miles distance from the shore.
About fifty Indians from the village came up to them.
The Indians wore stately mantels made of cotton.
The Indians appeared friendly disposed and to be caziques.
The Indians asked what their business was by signs.
The Indians pointed to the rising sun and asked if they came from that quarter.
The Indians pronounced the word 'Castilan.'
The Indians invited them to their village.
They held a consultation and unanimously agreed to follow the Indians.
The Indians took them to large edifices built of stone and lime.
The edifices were temples with walls covered with figures of snakes and gods.
They perceived fresh spots of blood around an altar.
Some idols had figures like crosses.
The paintings represented groups of Indians.
They believed the inhabitants had been sacrificing Indians to their gods.
Great numbers of Indians with their wives received them with pleasing smiles.
The numbers of Indians gradually increased, causing them to fear hostility.
A number of Indians approached them clad in tattered cloaks.
The Indians carried bundles of dried reeds.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his companions accepted an invitation to follow Native Americans with utmost circumspection.
The structures they were taken to were temples made of stone and lime with walls covered in figures of snakes and gods.
Bernal Diaz and his companions had never seen or heard of such temples before.
They believed the inhabitants had been sacrificing Indians to their gods to gain power to overcome them.
Bernal Diaz and his companions became fearful as the number of Indians gradually increased.
Bernal Diaz and his companions had previously been attacked at Cape Cotoche.
The priests in New Spain were commonly termed 'Papas'.
The priests used copal incense for perfuming.
The priests signaled that the Spanish should leave before bundles of reeds were consumed or they would be attacked and killed.
Bernal Diaz and his companions had received wounds at Cape Cotoche that were not yet healed.
Two of their men had died from wounds received at Cape Cotoche and were thrown overboard.
Bernal Diaz and his companions retreated to the shore in organized order.
They sailed for six days and six nights with fine weather.
A north wind brought stormy weather that lasted twenty-four hours.
They nearly perished in the storm and cast anchor near shore.
The safety of their ship depended on two ropes during the storm.
Bernal Diaz believed Providence preserved them through their old ropes and cables.
Their water casks were old and leaky.
They landed at an inner harbour near the village of Potonchan.
The distance from the harbour to Potonchan was about three miles.
They found wells, maize plantations, and stone buildings at the landing site.
They were attacked by inhabitants while filling their water casks.
The inhabitants of Potonchan wore cotton cuirasses reaching to their knees.
The inhabitants' swords were shaped like broadswords and wielded with both hands.
The inhabitants' bodies were painted with white, brown, and black colours.
The inhabitants approached in profound silence initially.
The inhabitants asked if the Spanish came from the rising of the sun, using the words 'Castilan, Castilan'.
The inhabitants of St. Lazaro had previously used the identical words 'Castilan, Castilan'.
Bernal Diaz and his companions posted watches and kept sharp lookout.
Bernal Diaz advised attacking the enemy that very night rather than retreating.
Bernal Diaz believed each of their men would have to encounter at least thirty Indians.
Bernal Diaz and his companions put their trust in God before the battle.
More than eighty of their men were wounded at the first onset of the battle.
The captain was wounded in twelve different places by arrows.
Bernal Diaz himself was wounded by three arrows, one in his left side piercing to the bone.
Two of their men were carried off alive: Alonzo Bote and an old Portuguese.
The enemy constantly received fresh troops, meat, drink, and arrows during the battle.
More than fifty of their men were killed in the battle.
Their boats sank or capsized as they rushed to them.
The Indians pursued them in canoes and attacked without intermission.
Fifty-seven of their men were missing after the battle.
Five men had died from wounds and extreme thirst and were thrown overboard.
The battle lasted a little longer than half an hour.
The battle took place at Potonchan.
Seamen named the location 'Bahea de mala Pelea' (the bay of the disastrous engagement).
Bernal Diaz and his companions returned thanks to God for their preservation.
Their wounded suffered greatly as they had only salt water to wash wounds.
Some men swore bitterly against chief pilot Alaminos for maintaining the land was an island.
None of the men escaped without two, three, or four wounds.
Only one single soldier came off whole from the battle.
They determined to return to Cuba after the battle.
They set fire to their smallest vessel due to insufficient hands to work the sails.
They distributed ropes, sails, and anchors from the smallest vessel to the other two.
They failed to bring off the water barrels and casks filled near Potonchan.
They suffered intensely from thirst on the return journey.
They held the edges of axes between their lips to refresh their parched tongues.
Bernal Diaz believed only those with experience could understand the hardships of discovering new countries.
They kept close to shore looking for streams or creeks with fresh water.
After three days they found an inlet they thought was a river mouth.
Fifteen sailors and three slightly wounded soldiers were sent ashore to find water.
The water in the inlet was salt and unfit for use.
Two soldiers became ill from drinking the salty water.
They named the location 'Lizard Bay' because the water swarmed with lizards.
A violent tempest from the north-east nearly cast their ships on shore.
The men on shore hastened to assist by casting out additional anchors and cables.
They lay anchored for two days and two nights during the tempest.
Pilot Alaminos consulted with two other pilots about their course.
The pilots concluded the best plan was to reach the latitude of Florida.
Florida was estimated to be no more than 210 miles distant according to their charts.
Alaminos had previously accompanied Juan de Leon when he discovered Florida about ten or twelve years earlier.
After four days' sail they were heading toward the Havannah.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was among twenty men who went on shore in Florida in search of water.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was slightly wounded in the right arm during the encounter with Florida natives.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo participated in a voyage of discovery that resulted in the expedition spending all their resources.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo returned to Cuba covered with wounds and poor after the voyage of discovery.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo remained behind in Havannah while recovering from wounds.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo traveled with three companions and Pedro d'Avila by canoe from Havannah to Trinidad.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo paid ten doubloons for passage on the canoe voyage.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo survived a shipwreck when the canoe was dashed to pieces on rocks.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo traveled barefoot over rocky terrain from the shipwreck site to Trinidad.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo fashioned coverings for his feet using tree bark and climber tendrils.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo had an acquaintance named Antonio de Medina in Trinidad who provided him with clothes.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo traveled to Santiago de Cuba to meet Governor Diego Velasquez.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was related to Governor Diego Velasquez.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo questioned the name 'Yucatan' given to the newly discovered country.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo believed the natives did not call their country Yucatan.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was asked by Diego Velasquez if he would join another expedition to Yucatan.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo smiled when asked about joining another expedition despite his recent hardships.
The pilot Alaminos had previously accompanied Juan de Leon when Florida was discovered.
The pilot Alaminos was severely wounded in the throat during the Florida encounter.
The captain of the expedition was dangerously wounded and weakened by extreme thirst.
The captain died ten days after arriving at Santispiritus from his wounds.
Six men were wounded at the first onset of the Indian attack in Florida.
Four sailors were wounded during the fight over the boat in Florida.
Twenty-two Indians lay dead on the shore after the skirmish.
A soldier named Berrio was lost and presumed killed by Indians while cutting a palm tree.
Berrio was the only man who escaped without a wound at the battle of Potonchan.
Three Indians taken aboard died soon after the Florida encounter.
A soldier died from drinking too much water too quickly after the voyage.
Three more men died of their wounds at Havannah.
The total loss of the expedition amounted to seventy men.
Two Indians named Melchorillo and Julianillo were brought from Punta de Cotoche.
Gold items and idols from the voyage became topics of conversation throughout the islands.
The fame of the discoveries reached Spain.
The two Indians falsely claimed there were gold mines in their country.
The two Indians claimed the cassava plant grew in their country and was called 'tale'.
The name 'Yucatan' arose from the words 'yuca' and 'tale' spoken by the Indians.
Governor Diego Velasquez boasted of the discoveries to Spanish officials.
The bishop of Burgos did not mention the names of the actual discoverers in his reports.
Diego Velasquez was fitting out another squadron in 1518.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo participated in the discovery of new countries.
Diego Velasquez was the governor of Cuba.
In 1518, Diego Velasquez sent out another expedition after learning a good account of the newly-discovered country called Yucatan.
Two of the four vessels in the 1518 expedition had been purchased at the warriors' own expense from a previous voyage to Yucatan under Cordoba.
Juan de Grijalva was related to Diego Velasquez.
Juan de Grijalva was appointed captain-general of the 1518 expedition.
Pedro de Alvarado, Francisco de Montejo, and Alonso de Avila were appointed to command the three other ships.
The commanders had to furnish vessels with provisions of cassava bread and pickled meat.
Diego Velasquez procured the four vessels and furnished crossbows, muskets, and goods for barter.
Reports of stone and lime houses in the newly-discovered country spread ideas of its riches.
The Indian Melchorejo indicated by signs that the country abounded in gold mines.
The 1518 expedition mustered 220 men.
Penolosa accompanied the expedition as comptroller.
Juan Diaz served as priest on the expedition.
Anton de Alaminos was the chief pilot.
Pedro de Alvarado subsequently became governor and chief justice of Guatemala, Honduras, and Chaopo.
Francisco de Montejo subsequently became governor and chief justice of Yucatan.
The four vessels lay in the harbor of Matanza on the north coast of Cuba.
Matanza harbor was near the old Havannah, which at that time was not built where it now stands.
The harbor of Matanza got its name from a massacre of Spanish shipwrecked sailors by Indians.
Thirty Spaniards and two Spanish ladies were shipwrecked near Matanza harbor.
Indians upset canoes in the river and drowned all but three men and one woman from the shipwrecked vessel.
Bernal Diaz was personally acquainted with the Spanish woman who survived the Matanza massacre.
The surviving Spanish woman married Pedro Sanchez Farsan, a citizen of Trinidad.
Bernal Diaz was acquainted with the three Spaniards whose lives were spared in the Matanza massacre.
Gonzalo Mexia was an old man and native of Xeres.
Juan de Santisteban was from Madrigal.
Cascorro was a sailor and fisherman from Huelva.
On April 5, 1518, the expedition departed after attending mass.
After ten days' sail, the expedition passed the cape of Guaniguanico.
Eight days after passing the cape, the expedition came in sight of the island of Cozumel on the feast of the Holy Cross.
The ships were carried further off by currents than during Cordoba's previous voyage.
The expedition landed on the south coast of Cozumel.
The inhabitants of Cozumel fled when they saw the vessels approaching.
Julianillo and Melchorejo had been captured at Punta de Cotoche.
The distance between Cozumel and the mainland was only four hours' sail.
The inhabitants of Cozumel spoke the same language as the mainland inhabitants.
An Indian woman from Jamaica came to the expedition and spoke the language of Jamaica and Cuba.
The Indian woman from Jamaica had left Jamaica two years prior in a large canoe to fish.
The Indian woman's husband and most of her companions were killed and sacrificed by the inhabitants of Cozumel.
The expedition called the place Santa Cruz because it was discovered four or five days before the feast of the Holy Cross.
Cozumel had plenty of honey, manioc, potatoes, and large herds of musk swine.
Cozumel contained three poor villages.
The Indian woman from Jamaica went along with the expedition.
The expedition sailed for eight days before arriving off the coast of Champoton.
At Champoton, the Indians had previously killed fifty-six Spanish men and wounded all the rest.
The Indians at Champoton were well armed with bows, lances, shields, and broadswords.
The Indians at Champoton had slings, cotton cuirasses, drums, and trumpets.
Most of the Indians at Champoton had their faces painted black and white.
The expedition brought falconets, crossbows, and matchlocks to the battle at Champoton.
Juan de Quitera was killed in the conflict at Champoton.
Juan de Grijalva received three arrow wounds and lost two of his teeth at Champoton.
Above sixty men were wounded in the conflict at Champoton.
The expedition made three prisoners at Champoton, one of whom was a person of rank.
The expedition stayed four days at Champoton.
Immense-sized locusts were seen at Champoton during the battle.
The expedition mistook locusts for arrows and arrows for locusts during the battle at Champoton.
The expedition discovered an opening on the coast that appeared to be a broad river.
Anton de Alaminos asserted that the opening was an island with two promontories.
The opening was named Boca de Terminos.
The expedition explored Terminos bay for three whole days.
Terminos was a deep indented bay formed by the continent.
Temples built of stone and lime were found at Terminos.
The temples at Terminos contained idols made of wood or clay.
The district of Terminos was quite uninhabited.
Fallow deer and rabbits abounded at Terminos.
The expedition killed ten fallow deer and great numbers of rabbits with one greyhound at Terminos.
A greyhound ran away at Terminos and was later found in excellent condition when the expedition returned with Cortes.
The river Tabasco was later called the river Grijalva after its discoverer.
The expedition perceived a very broad mouth of a river on the third morning of sailing along the coast.
The waves broke over the shallows at the river mouth.
The two larger vessels could not enter the river and anchored further out at sea.
The two smaller vessels and all boats proceeded up the river.
Indians with bows and arrows were perceived in canoes along the shore of the river.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo sailed along the coast of the continent during the day and lay to at night due to shallows and rocks.
The expedition discovered a broad river mouth on the third morning of sailing.
Two larger vessels could not enter the river due to shallows.
Two smaller vessels that drew less water proceeded up the river with all boats well manned.
Indians with bows and arrows were perceived in canoes along the shore, armed similarly to those at Champoton.
The expedition took fish from basket kiddles and placed them in a boat.
The river was previously called Tabasco after the cazique of the district.
The expedition renamed the river Grijalva in honor of their chief commander.
The Indians were constructing barricades and preparing for war when the expedition heard the crackling noise of felling trees.
About fifty canoes with armed Indians made towards the expedition.
The expedition considered firing great guns and muskets but decided through merciful Providence to first try gaining the Indians' friendship.
Julianillo and Melchorejo, natives of Punta de Cotoche, served as interpreters.
The expedition showed the Indians necklaces of blue glass beads, small mirrors, and green imitation corals.
The Indians thought the green imitation corals were chalchihuis stones, which were highly esteemed in their country.
The captain told the Indians they were subjects of Emperor Don Carlos.
The Indians responded that they already had a master and could not accept a new one imposed upon them.
The Indians reported that two armies of 8000 men each were ready for action against the expedition.
The Indians mentioned they had learned the expedition killed and wounded 200 men at Potonchan.
The Indians embraced the commander in token of peace.
The caziques and papas declared that the offer of peace was acceptable to them.
The inhabitants of these countries customarily made each other presents when in treaty about peace.
About thirty Indians arrived with broiled fish, fowls, fruit, and maize-bread.
The Indians brought pans filled with red-hot embers on which they strewed incense.
The Indians presented gold ornaments in the shape of ducks and lizards, and three gold necklaces worth less than 200 pesos.
The Indians presented mantles and waistcoats similar to their own.
The Indians repeatedly mentioned the words Culba and Mexico, indicating gold was found further toward the setting sun.
The expedition rejoiced at the certainty that gold existed in the country.
The vessels were in great danger due to north winds.
The expedition perceived a town called Aguajaluco on the second day after re-embarking.
Indians at Aguajaluco carried huge shields made of tortoise-shells that glittered in the sun.
The expedition named the town La Rambla.
The expedition named a bay and river Sant Antonio after entering the bay where the river Tonola emptied.
The expedition passed the mouth of the great river Guacasualco but could not enter due to contrary winds.
The expedition came in sight of the Snow Mountains (Sierras Nevadas), which were covered with snow year-round.
A soldier named San Martino from Havannah first descried mountains near the coast, which were named Holy Martin in his honor.
Pedro de Alvarado ran his ship up a river called Papalohuna by the natives, which the expedition termed the Alvarado.
Indian fishermen of Tlacotalpa gave fish to Alvarado.
Grijalva forbade Alvarado to sail in advance of the squadron after this incident caused ill blood.
From that time forward, the four vessels kept close together.
The expedition arrived at the mouth of a river termed Bandera's Stream due to Indians bearing small flags of white cloth attached to their lances.
Motecusuma was the mighty monarch of the great city of Mexico.
Mexico was built in the water like Venice.
Motecusuma's power was so great he wished to extend it to impossible places.
Motecusuma had heard of the expedition's first visit under Cordoba and battles at Cotoche and Champoton.
Motecusuma knew the expedition was a handful of men compared to the multitude of inhabitants.
Motecusuma was aware the expedition gladly exchanged goods for gold.
Information was sent to Motecusuma by means of painted figures or signs drawn on cloth manufactured from maguey.
Motecusuma issued orders to governors of districts to exchange gold for glass beads, particularly green ones.
An ancient tradition in the country spoke of a people coming from the rising sun who would gain dominion of the country.
Motecusuma ordered sentinels to be posted along the banks of the river with small flags attached to their lances.
Francisco de Montejo commanded two boats manned with twenty soldiers armed with crossbows and muskets.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was among the soldiers sent to investigate the sentinels' meaning.
The weather was calm, which is seldom the case on these coasts.
Three caziques met the expedition on shore, one of whom was a governor under Motecusuma.
The Indians brought fowls, maize-bread, pines, sapotas, and other provisions.
Julianillo did not understand the language of these Indians.
The Indians perfumed the expedition with a species of resin smelling like incense.
The general resolved to run the whole squadron into the bay and proceed on shore with all men.
The caziques and governor paid the general the greatest possible respect.
Motecusuma's governor strongly advised the Indians to comply with the expedition's request for gold.
During six days at this spot, the expedition obtained upwards of 1500 pesos' worth of gold trinkets of inferior quality.
Francisco Lopez de Gomara and Gonzalo Hernandez de Oviedo mentioned this circumstance in their works.
These historians erred in saying the gold exchange took place in the Tabasco or Grijalva river.
There is no gold found in the provinces bordering the river Grijalva, and very few gold ornaments.
The expedition took possession of the country for Emperor Charles in the name of Diego Velasquez, governor of Cuba.
The general presented the Indians with Spanish shirts.
The expedition took one Indian who subsequently learned their language and was converted to Christianity, taking the name Francisco.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo met Francisco afterwards at Santa Fé after the conquest of Mexico.
The commander found the inhabitants brought no more gold after six days.
The anchorage was dangerous due to contrary winds.
The expedition came to an island covered with white sand, lying above nine miles from the mainland.
The expedition named this island Isla Blanca.
Another island lay nearly five miles from the mainland and offered a commodious landing-place.
The general ordered boats to be lowered to explore the island.
Two houses on the island were strongly built of stone and lime with flights of steps and altars.
Several abominable idols stood on the altars.
Five Indians had been sacrificed the previous evening, their bodies ripped open with arms and legs chopped off.
Everything near the sacrificed bodies was besmeared with blood.
The expedition named the island Isla de Sacrificios.
The expedition constructed huts on the neighboring continent using sails and branches of trees.
Numbers of Indians appeared bringing small pieces of gold for barter, according to Motecusuma's commands.
The inhabitants approached in great fear and brought only trifles.
The captain soon weighed anchor again.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo explored an island and found two stone and lime houses with altars.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his men discovered evidence of human sacrifice on the island they named Isla de Sacrificios.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his companions were astonished by the sight of sacrificed bodies.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his men constructed huts on the continent using sails and tree branches.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his company found Indians bringing small pieces of gold for barter.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was annoyed by multitudes of mosquitoes on the sandy coast.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was shocked by the sight of recently sacrificed boys at the temple.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his men refused to be perfumed by the priests due to their disgust at the sacrifices.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo's captain questioned an Indian named Francisco about the meaning of the sacrifices.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo's group named the island San Juan de Ulua after their commander Juan and the feast of St. John.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo remained seven days in the constructed huts despite mosquito harassment.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his men determined they had discovered a continent, not an island.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo's group had lost ten men from wounds and had four others dangerously ill.

The distance from Cozumel to Cotoche was nine miles.
Cortes wrote a letter to the Spaniards offering them honorable treatment and assistance.
Cortes mentioned having eleven vessels armed with 500 soldiers in his letter.
Cortes intended to proceed to Tabasco or Potonchon.
The vessel passed the narrow gulf in three hours.
Geronimo de Aguilar was one of the Spaniards held prisoner by the Indians.
Aguilar was rejoiced upon reading Cortes's letter and receiving the ransom-money.
Gonzalo Guerrero was Aguilar's comrade among the imprisoned Spaniards.
Guerrero had united himself to an Indian woman and had three children with her.
Guerrero held a position as good as cazique or chief during wartime.
Guerrero's face had been disfigured according to Indian custom.
Guerrero's ears had been pierced according to Indian custom.
Guerrero refused to leave with Aguilar despite Aguilar's persuasion.
Guerrero was concerned about appearing before his countrymen in his altered state.
Guerrero asked Aguilar for glass beads to give to his children.
Guerrero's Indian wife was displeased with Aguilar's errand.
Aguilar made another attempt to persuade Guerrero by appealing to his Christian faith.
Aguilar offered to take Guerrero's wife and children with him if Guerrero would leave.
Guerrero was probably a sailor and native of Palos.
Guerrero remained among the Indians while Aguilar departed.

A soldier named Berrio accused sailors of Gibraleon of stealing bacon from him.
The accused sailors denied the theft and took an oath.
The stolen bacon was found among the sailors' clothes after a search.
Seven sailors were involved in the bacon robbery.
Cortes ordered the seven sailors to be severely whipped despite their officers' intercession.
Cozumel was a place to which Indians made pilgrimages.
Neighboring tribes from Cotoche and other districts of Yucatan came to Cozumel to sacrifice to idols.
The Indians burned a resin that resembled Christian incense.

Cortes found the priest's sermon tended to ungodliness.
Cortes ordered the caziques, principal men, and priest into his presence.
Cortes told the Indians they must give up sacrificing to idols if they wished to become his brethren.
Cortes presented the Indians with an image of the Virgin Mary and a cross.

Cortes explained many things respecting Christian religion to the Indians.
The caziques and priests answered that their forefathers had prayed to their idols.

Cortes ordered lime to be collected to construct an altar.

The image of the holy Virgin was placed on the new altar.
Alonso Yañez and Alvaro Lopez, two carpenters, made a cross of new wood.
A chapel was built behind the altar to house the cross.
Father Juan Diaz said mass in front of the new altar.
The caziques and priests watched the mass with great attention.

Cortes commanded the principal vessel over the whole squadron.
Cortes appointed Alvarado and his brother to command the San Sebastian.
Alonso Hernandez Puertocarrero commanded one of the vessels.
Francisco de Montejo commanded one of the vessels.
Christobal de Oli commanded one of the vessels.
Diego de Ordas commanded one of the vessels.
Juan Velasquez de Leon commanded one of the vessels.
Juan de Escalante commanded one of the vessels.
Francisco de Morla commanded one of the vessels.
Escobar the page commanded one of the vessels.
Gines Nortes commanded the smallest vessel, a brigantine that he owned.
Every vessel had its own pilot who received instructions from Alaminos.
Alaminos provided signals with lanterns to the pilots.

Cortes commended the caziques and priests to the image of the holy Virgin and the cross.
Cortes desired the Indians to pray before the image and cross.
Cortes instructed the Indians not to damage the image and cross but to decorate them with green boughs.

The Spanish forces set sail with favorable winds.

The cassava-bread was unloaded from the sinking vessel.

The caziques and priests asked what caused the Spanish forces to return so speedily.

Cortes asked the Indians to assist with their canoes in unloading cassava-bread.
The Indians readily complied with Cortes's request.
It took four more days to repair the vessel.
Cortes was the commander of the Spanish expedition.
A soldier named Zaragoza reported that Juan de Escalante's vessel laden with cassava-bread was sinking.
Alaminos was the chief pilot of the expedition.
The Spanish expedition returned to the island of Cozumel to repair a leaky vessel.
An image of the holy Virgin and a cross were found in good condition upon return to Cozumel.
The caziques and priests of Cozumel assisted the Spanish in unloading cassava-bread.
It took four days to repair the vessel.
Geronimo de Aguilar was a Spaniard who had been in the power of Indians.
Aguilar hired a canoe with six rowers to reach the island of Cozumel.
The channel between the island and mainland was about twelve miles.
Andreas de Tapia was dispatched by Cortes to learn news from the arriving canoe.
Aguilar initially appeared to be an Indian due to his appearance.
Aguilar's complexion was naturally brownish.
Aguilar's hair had been shorn like that of an Indian slave.
Aguilar carried a paddle across his shoulder.
Aguilar wore tattered clothing and carried a torn prayerbook.
Cortes provided Aguilar with a shirt, coat, trousers, cap, and shoes.
Aguilar was a native of Ecija.
Aguilar had been shipwrecked about eight years before meeting Cortes.
Aguilar was shipwrecked with fifteen men and two women.
The shipwreck occurred on a voyage between Darien and the island of St. Domingo.
The voyage was undertaken due to a lawsuit between Enciso and Valdivia.
The ship carried 10,000 pesos and papers relating to the lawsuit.
The ship struck against a rock and could not be refloated.
The crew attempted to reach Cuba or Jamaica by boat.
The Calachionies captured the crew and distributed them among themselves.
Most of Aguilar's companions were sacrificed to Indian gods.
Both women in Aguilar's group died from hard labor and grief.
Aguilar was also destined to be sacrificed but escaped during the night.
Aguilar fled to a cazique whose name he could not remember.
Gonzalo Guerrero was Aguilar's only surviving companion.
Aguilar attempted to convince Guerrero to leave but failed.
Aguilar had been treated as a slave, fetching wood and water and working in maize plantations.
Aguilar was once sent on business about twelve miles from his village but could not complete the journey.
Aguilar understood the country to be very thickly populated.
Gonzalo Guerrero had married an Indian woman and fathered three children.
Guerrero had adopted Indian customs including tattooed cheeks and pierced ears.
Guerrero was a sailor by profession and native of Palos.
Guerrero was considered by Indians to be a man of great strength.
A squadron of three vessels had touched at Cotoche about a year before Aguilar's rescue.
Guerrero advised the inhabitants of Cotoche to commence hostilities against the Spanish.
Cortes wished to capture Guerrero because his presence among Indians would harm Spanish interests.
The caziques of Cozumel showed Aguilar friendship when he spoke their language.
Aguilar advised the caziques to honor the image of the holy Virgin and cross.
The caziques requested letters of recommendation from Cortes to protect them from other Spanish.
Cortes complied with the caziques' request for letters of recommendation.
The historian Gomara gave a different account of Aguilar than what Aguilar himself told.
On March 4, 1519, Cortes ordered the expedition to re-embark.
The expedition experienced favorable weather initially after leaving Cozumel.
The weather suddenly changed with violent winds endangering the vessels.
The wind abated towards midnight.
Velasquez de Leon's vessel became separated from the fleet.
Cortes ordered the fleet to drop anchor to search for the missing vessel.
Manquillo was the pilot of Velasquez de Leon's vessel.
Manquillo had previously visited the seas with Hernandez de Cordoba and Grijalva.
The missing vessel was found anchored in a bay.
Francisco de Lugo was one of the principal officers of the expedition.
Alaminos and Lugo went ashore and found inhabited country with maize plantations.
The explorers found places where salt was manufactured.
Four large temples with numerous female figures were found at the promontory.
The promontory was called la punta de las Mujeres (the promontory of women).
Aguilar had once been a slave at la punta de las Mujeres.
Alonso Guerrero had settled in a township near la punta de las Mujeres.
Every inhabitant of the area possessed gold in small quantities.
Cortes stated he had not come for the sake of gold but to serve God and his king.
Cortes dispatched Escobar to examine Terminos bay and search for a suitable colony location.
Escobar was instructed to leave signs indicating he had explored the harbor safely.
Escobar found a greyhound that had run away from the Spanish during Grijalva's expedition.
The greyhound was glossy, fat, and recognized the ship.
Escobar was driven out to sea by a strong south wind.
The Spanish found trees cut down and a written note from Escobar at Terminos bay.
Escobar's note indicated the bay and country were charming and abounded with game.
The Spanish arrived at Potonchan and Cortes ordered Alaminos to run into the inlet.
Alaminos declared the waters off Potonchan were too shallow for the vessels.
Cortes intended to punish the inhabitants of Potonchan severely.
Many Spanish who had been present at previous engagements at Potonchan wanted revenge.
Alaminos and other pilots warned that running into Potonchan would delay the expedition by three to eight days.
The wind was favorable for reaching the Tabasco river.
The expedition reached the Tabasco river after three days' sail.
On March 12, 1519, the expedition arrived at the mouth of the Tabasco.
Larger vessels anchored at sea while smaller vessels sailed up the river.
The Spanish disembarked at a promontory with palm trees about four miles from the town of Tabasco.
This was the same spot where Grijalva had previously landed.
More than 12,000 armed Indians assembled at the town of Tabasco to attack the Spanish.
The town of Tabasco was very powerful with many subject tribes.
The inhabitants of Potonchan and Lazaro had accused the Tabascans of cowardice.
The Tabascans were reproached for giving Grijalva gold trinkets.
The tribes of Potonchan and Lazaro had killed fifty-six Spanish men.
The Tabascans took up arms partly due to reproaches from neighboring tribes.
Cortes used Aguilar to communicate with the Tabascans.
Cortes offered to treat the Tabascans as brothers and share victuals with them.
The Tabascans became more insolent despite Aguilar's peaceful overtures.
The Tabascans threatened to destroy the Spanish if they set foot on their territory.
The Tabascans had fortified their town with felled trees and a stone wall.
Aguilar made multiple attempts to negotiate peace with the Tabascans.
The Tabascans refused to allow the Spanish to pass beyond the palm trees.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo participated in an expedition to Tabasco.
The Spanish attempted to make peace with the Indians of Tabasco before engaging in battle.
Aguilar served as an interpreter between the Spanish and the Indians.
The Spanish offered to share their victuals with the Indians.
The Indians of Tabasco fortified their territory with felled trees and a stone wall.
Cortes ordered the preparation of small vessels and boats for battle.
Three pieces of cannon were placed on board each vessel.
Crossbow-men and musketeers were distributed among the vessels.
Cortes had previously learned of a narrow road to the town during Grijalva's expedition.
Cortes posted three sentinels to watch for Indian movements.
The Spanish attended mass before the battle.
Alonso de Avila was dispatched with one hundred men along a narrow road to attack the town.
Cortes himself moved up the river in small vessels and boats.
The Indians used twisted shells, drums, and fifes to make noise.
The royal secretary and Diego de Godoy were sent to request peaceful landing.
The Indians responded to Spanish requests with defiance and threats.
The battle began with the Indians pouring forth showers of arrows.
The Spanish were forced to fight in water up to their waists and sometimes higher.
The landing ground was composed of mud and clay.
Cortes lost one of his shoes in the mud during the battle.
The Spanish fought under the cry of their patron St. Jacob.
The Indians retreated but regrouped behind fallen trees and wood.
The Spanish forced passages leading to the town and entered it fighting.
The battle continued in the streets of the town.
The Indians cried out 'ala lala, al calachoni' meaning 'kill the commander-in-chief.'
Alonso de Avila was delayed by morass and pools of water.
The Spanish found three temples in a large courtyard.
The Indians had carried off all religious implements from the temples.
Cortes took formal possession of the country in the name of his majesty.
Cortes drew his sword and made two deep cuts into a large ceiba tree.
All present swore to support Cortes in defending the country.
The royal treasurer formally registered the proceedings.
The adherents of Diego Velasquez were displeased that his name was not mentioned.
Fourteen Spanish men were wounded in the engagement.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was wounded by an arrow in the thigh.
The Indians lost eighteen men in the engagement.
The Spanish posted sentinels during the night.
Cortes dispatched Alvarado with one hundred men to explore the interior.
Alvarado's party included fifteen crossbow-men and musketeers.
Melchorejo of the punta de Cotoche was supposed to accompany Alvarado but disappeared.
Melchorejo left his Spanish clothes hanging in a tree.
Cortes was greatly vexed at Melchorejo's escape.
Cortes sent Francisco de Lugo with one hundred men in another direction.
Francisco de Lugo's party included twelve crossbow-men and musketeers.
Francisco de Lugo encountered vast numbers of Indians commanded by their chiefs.
De Lugo's men were surrounded and attacked with arrows, lances, and swords.
De Lugo's men retreated in order to headquarters.
An Indian of Cuba was sent as a swift runner to inform Cortes.
Alvarado heard the firing of muskets and noise of drums and trumpets.
Alvarado marched to assist De Lugo in his engagement.
Five Indians were killed before Alvarado's arrival.
Two of De Lugo's detachment were killed in the engagement.
Eight of De Lugo's men were wounded.
Alvarado had three men wounded.
Fifteen Indians were killed in the battle.
Three Indians were taken prisoners.
One of the prisoners appeared to be a chief.
Melchorejo had advised the Indians to attack the Spanish.
Melchorejo told the Indians the Spanish could be conquered due to their small numbers.
The Spanish sent one prisoner to the caziques with green glass beads and offers of peace.
The prisoner sent to the caziques never returned.
All the caziques of neighboring districts had been under arms to attack the Spanish.
The caziques planned to storm the Spanish head-quarters the next day.
Cortes ordered all horses to be brought on shore.
The horses appeared awkward and fearful after being at sea.
The horses regained their liveliness and agility the following day.
Six or seven young Spanish men were attacked with severe pains in the groins.
The Spanish attributed the groin pains to living freely at Cuba and the heat.
Cortes ordered the afflicted men back on board.
Cavaliers were commanded to hang bells around their horses' necks.
Cortes instructed cavalry not to rush at Indians with lances before they were dispersed.
Cortes instructed cavalry to aim only at the Indians' faces.
Christobal de Oli was selected to fight on horseback.
Pedro de Alvarado was selected to fight on horseback.
Alonso Hernandez Puertocarrero was selected to fight on horseback.
Juan de Escalante was selected to fight on horseback.
Francisco de Montejo and Alonso de Avila used the horses of Ortiz the musician and Bartolome Garcia.
Velasquez de Leon was a superior horseman.
Francisco de Morla was a superior horseman.
One of the Lares was an excellent horseman.
Gonzalo Dominiguez was a superior horseman.
Moron de Bayamo was selected to fight on horseback.
Pedro de Truxillo was selected to fight on horseback.
Cortes placed himself at the head of the cavalry.
Mesa had charge of the artillery.
Diego de Ordas commanded the rest of the men.
Diego de Ordas excelled as a crossbow-man and musketeer.
The Spanish attended mass on the day of annunciation to the holy Virgin.
Antonio de Villareal carried the ensign.
The Spanish marched towards extensive bean fields.
The village of Cintla belonged to the Tabascans.
Cintla lay about four miles from the Spanish head-quarters.
Cortes took a circuitous route due to bogs that horses could not pass.
Diego de Ordas led the other troops near Cintla.
The Indians arranged themselves on the plain near Cintla.
The Indians wore large bunches of feathers on their heads.
The Indians wore cotton cuirasses.
The Indians' faces were daubed with white, black, and red colors.
The Indians were armed with huge bows and arrows, shields, lances, and large broadswords.
The Indians had bodies of slingers.
The Indians had warriors armed with poles hardened in the fire.
The Indians completely filled the bean fields.
The Indians fell upon the Spanish on all sides at once.
Above seventy Spanish men were wounded in the initial attack.
A Spanish man named Saldaña was struck by an arrow in the ear and died instantly.
The Spanish repaid the Indians with crossbows, muskets, and heavy cannon.
The Spanish cut right and left among the Indians with their swords.
Mesa made terrible havoc among the Indians with cannon fire.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo remarked to Diego de Ordas that they should rush forward and close with the Indians.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo observed that the Indians retreated from fear of Spanish swords.
Diego de Ordas initially considered rushing forward not expedient due to the enemy's vast numbers.
The Spanish eventually fell heavily upon the Indians.
The Indians retreated as far as the wells.
Cortes remained behind with the cavalry during the initial engagement.
The Spanish longed for cavalry reinforcement during the battle.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo advised Diego de Ordas to rush forward and close with the Indians in combat.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo observed that the Indians retreated from fear of Spanish swords but continued to attack from a distance with arrows, lances, and stones.
Diego de Ordas initially considered it inexpedient to rush forward because the enemy's numbers were so vast.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo's advice to rush forward was eventually followed.
Cortes remained behind with the cavalry while Bernal Diaz del Castillo and others fought the Indians.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo longed for cavalry reinforcement during the battle.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo feared that some misfortune might have befallen Cortes.
The Indians made piping and yelling sounds at every cannon shot.
The Indians attempted to hide their losses by tossing up earth and straw into the air.
The Indians used drums, trumpets, and a war-whoop called 'Ala lala' during battle.
Cortes arrived with cavalry and attacked the Indians from behind.
The Indians had never seen horses before and thought horse and rider were one body.
Cortes was delayed by a morass before arriving with cavalry.
Cortes fought his way through other enemy bodies and had five men and eight horses wounded.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his companions praised God and the holy Virgin after the victory.
The battle occurred on the feast of the Annunciation to the holy Virgin.
The town subsequently built in memory of this victory was named Santa Maria de la Vitoria.
This was the first battle Bernal Diaz del Castillo fought under Cortes in New Spain.
Spanish soldiers bandaged their wounds with linen.
Spanish soldiers dressed horse wounds with melted fat cut from dead Indian bodies.
The Spanish found above eight hundred killed Indians on the field of battle.
Spanish swords did the most carnage among the Indians.
The cavalry's appearance caused the enemy the most difficulty.
The fighting lasted about an hour.
The Spanish took five prisoners, including two caziques.
Two Spanish soldiers were killed, one shot in the neck and the other in the ear.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo and his companions were fatigued and hungry after the battle.
Francisco Lopez de Gomara claimed that St. Jacob or St. Peter had galloped to their assistance on a gray-colored horse.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo attributes the victory to Jesus Christ and God's mercy.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo did not see the apostles himself, only Francisco de Morla on a brown horse.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo never heard any of the four hundred soldiers, Cortes, or any cavaliers mention the apostles' appearance.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo considers himself an unworthy sinner.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo would have been delighted if Gomara's account of the apostles was true.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo never heard the wonder of the apostles mentioned before reading Gomara's book.
Aguilar understood the Indian language and discoursed with the prisoners.
Cortes proposed setting the prisoners at liberty and sending them as delegates to their countrymen.
Cortes ordered the prisoners to be presented with blue glass beads.
Aguilar assured the prisoners that the Indians had nothing further to fear from the Spanish after the battle.
The Indians sent fifteen Indian slaves with fowls, baked fish, and maize-bread as a response to the peace message.
The Indian slaves had their faces blackened and were covered with ragged cloaks.
Aguilar asked the blackfaced Indians in an angry tone why they came seeking war rather than peace.
Aguilar told the blackfaced Indians that persons of rank should be deputed, not slaves.
The Spanish treated the blackfaced Indians kindly and presented them with blue beads.
The following day, above thirty principal Indians appeared in Spanish quarters, well dressed.
The Indians requested permission to burn and bury their fallen countrymen.
Cortes granted permission for the Indians to burn and bury their dead.
The Indians reported they had lost above 800 killed without counting the wounded.
The Indians said they could not enter into a treaty until all chiefs and principal personages assembled.
Cortes believed the Indians stood in great awe of Spanish horses.
Cortes devised a plan to further confirm the Indians' belief in the power of horses.
Cortes ordered the mare of Juan Sedeño to be brought and fastened where he stood.
Cortes ordered the stallion of the musician Ortiz to be brought, which was a very fiery animal.
Cortes ordered the largest cannon to be heavily loaded with gunpowder and ball.
Forty caziques arrived in great state and richly clothed according to their fashion.
The caziques saluted Cortes and the Spanish soldiers and perfumed them with incense.
The caziques begged forgiveness for what had happened and promised to be friendly.
Cortes reminded the caziques through Aguilar how often he had wished them to make peace.
Cortes told the caziques they were vassals of the emperor Charles.
Cortes fired the largest cannon as a demonstration to the caziques.
The cannon's report sounded like a sudden clap of thunder.
The caziques appeared in dismay at the cannon fire.
The stallion neighed, stamped the ground, and reared itself when brought near the caziques.
The caziques thought the stallion's movements were directed against them.
Cortes walked to the horse and took hold of the bridle to calm the caziques.
Aguilar made the Indians believe Cortes had ordered the horse not to harm them.
Above thirty Indian porters arrived with fowls, baked fish, and various fruits.
The caziques left the Spanish quarters perfectly contented with assurances of returning the next day with a present.

The Indians brought four diadems, lizards, ear-rings, four ducks, figures like dogs, figures with Indian faces, two sandals with golden soles, and various gold trinkets.
The Indians brought cloaks as they wear them, which are very commodious.
The present was of little value, likely because the province possessed few riches.
The Indians presented the Spanish with twenty females.
One of the females was a very fine woman who subsequently became a convert to Christianity and was named Doña Marina.
Cortes was vastly pleased with the present of the twenty females.
Cortes told the caziques their present was acceptable but asked them to return to their dwellings with their wives and children.
Cortes said he would not consider the peace concluded unless all inhabitants returned to the village within two days.
The caziques issued orders and all families returned within two days.
The Indians showed readiness to comply with Cortes's wishes to do away with their idols and human sacrifices.
Cortes gave the Indians some idea of Christian faith and explained that the Spanish adored only one God.
The Spanish showed the Indians a pious figure of the mother of God holding her blessed Son.
The Spanish explained they paid reverence to this figure and to the mother of God in heaven.
The caziques said they were pleased with the figure and called it 'Tecleciguata,' meaning a woman of distinction.
The caziques wanted to keep the figure of the mother of God in their village.
Cortes promised the caziques they should have the figure and ordered a pretty altar to be built.
The carpenters Alonso Yañez and Alvaro Lopez were ordered to construct a very high cross.
Cortes asked the caziques why they had commenced war with the Spanish for the third time.
The caziques answered they were sorry and had been forgiven.
The cazique of Champoton had instigated the attacks and accused the Tabasco Indians of cowardice.
The cazique of Champoton said the Tabasco Indians should have attacked when the Spanish arrived with four ships under another commander, meaning Grijalva.
The Spanish Indian interpreter had run away and advised the Indians not to leave the Spanish any peace.
Cortes desired the runaway interpreter to be delivered up to him.
The caziques declared they did not know what had become of the runaway interpreter.
The runaway interpreter was seized and sacrificed to the Indians' gods after the battle.
The Indians said they got their gold and trinkets from the country towards the setting of the sun.
The Indians pronounced the words 'Culhua' and 'Mexico' when asked about the source of their gold.
The Spanish paid little attention to the words 'Culhua' and 'Mexico' at that time.
Francisco, the other Spanish interpreter from Grijalva's expedition, remained with the Spanish.
Francisco knew very little of the Tabasco language but was acquainted with the Culhuan, or Mexican language.
Francisco told Cortes by signs that 'Culhua' lay at a great distance before them.
Francisco continually mentioned the word 'Mexico' to Cortes.
The Spanish were ignorant of what Francisco wished to convey about Mexico.
The day following the arrival of the caziques, the cross and altar were erected.
The figure of the holy Virgin was placed on the altar.
The Spanish fell down upon their knees before the figure of the holy Virgin.
Father Bartolome de Olmedo read mass.
The caziques and chief Indians were present at the mass.
Bernal Diaz del Castillo was present at the battle of Tabasco.
An advisor to the Spanish was seized and sacrificed to the gods shortly after the battle.
The Spanish initially did not understand the meaning of the words 'Culhua' and 'Mexico' when the Indians mentioned them.
Francisco was an interpreter who remained with the Spanish from Grijalva's former expedition.
Francisco knew very little of the Tabasco language and was only acquainted with the Culhuan or Mexican language.
A cross and altar were erected the day after the battle.
Father Bartolome de Olmedo read mass at the erected altar.
The village of Tabasco was named Santa Maria de la Vitoria.
Twenty female Indians were presented to the Spanish.
Father Olmedo and Aguilar addressed the women about Christian religion.
The women were baptized.
One of the baptized women was named Doña Marina.
Doña Marina was a lady of distinction and daughter of a powerful cazique and princess.
Doña Marina was the prettiest, most active and lively of the baptized women.

Cortes had a son by Doña Marina named Don Martin Cortes.

The Spanish remained five days in Tabasco to cure wounds and treat those suffering from pain in the groins.
Cortes spoke with the caziques about the emperor and the advantages of subjection to him.
The caziques declared themselves vassals of the Spanish emperor.
These were the first inhabitants of New Spain to subject themselves to the emperor's majesty.
The day following was Palm Sunday.
Cortes sent for six Indian carpenters to assist in making a cross on a high ceiba tree near the village of Cintla.
The cross was made to be very durable by cutting the bark of the tree in the shape of a cross.
Cortes desired the Indians to bring out their canoes to assist in re-embarking.
The Spanish desired to set sail on Palm Sunday because their station was not secure from north winds.
The caziques and principal personages appeared with their wives and children for the Palm Sunday procession.
Father Bartolome de Olmedo and Juan Dias were dressed in full canonicals and read mass.
The Spanish prayed before the cross and kissed it during the ceremony.
The principal Indians brought ten fowls, baked fish, and greens for the Spanish to eat.
Cortes recommended the Indians take care of the image of the holy Virgin and the cross.
The Spanish embarked in the evening and set sail on Monday morning with a good wind.
The Spanish kept close to the shore and steered toward San Juan de Ulua.
Those who had been with Grijalva pointed out various geographical features to Cortes as they coasted along.
The Spanish arrived at San Juan de Ulua on Holy Thursday about noon.
Alonso Hernandez Puertocarrero remarked to Cortes about arriving in a rich country.
Cortes responded by invoking God's fortune in arms and Roland the Paladin.
Doña Marina was born a ruler over a people and country.
Doña Marina's parents had dominion of a township called Painala with several subject townships about twenty-four miles from Guacasualco.
Doña Marina's father died when she was very young.
Doña Marina's mother remarried another young cazique.
Doña Marina's mother and stepfather had a son to whom they designed to leave their territories.
Doña Marina was conveyed secretly at night to an Indian family in Xicalango to prevent her from standing in her half-brother's way.
A rumor was spread that Doña Marina had died.
A daughter of one of Doña Marina's female slaves died at the time, which gave credence to the rumor.
